questions.stem: A topology on a set $X$ is a collection $\tau$ of subsets of $X$ such that:
options: $\varnothing$ and $X$ lie in $\tau$, and $\tau$ is closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.
$\tau$ is closed under arbitrary unions and arbitrary intersections.
$\tau$ contains exactly the finite subsets of $X$.
questions.explanation: The axioms for a topology require that $\varnothing, X \in \tau$, that any union of members of $\tau$ is in $\tau$, and that any finite intersection of members of $\tau$ is in $\tau$. Arbitrary intersections need not stay open.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A map $f : X \to Y$ between topological spaces is continuous when:
options: $f$ sends open sets to open sets.
the preimage $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$ for every open $V \subseteq Y$.
$f$ sends closed sets to closed sets.
questions.explanation: Continuity is defined by the requirement that preimages of open sets are open. Sending open sets to open sets is the definition of an open map, a different notion.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A homeomorphism $f : X \to Y$ is:
options: a continuous bijection whose inverse is also continuous.
questions.explanation: A homeomorphism is a continuous bijection with continuous inverse. A continuous bijection alone need not be a homeomorphism, for instance the identity from a finer to a coarser topology. Distance preservation is an isometry, a stronger metric notion.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Which property is a topological invariant, that is, preserved by every homeomorphism?
questions.explanation: Compactness is preserved by homeomorphisms (indeed by continuous surjections). Diameter and boundedness are metric notions that depend on a chosen metric, not on the topology alone.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The $n$-sphere $S^n$ is usually defined as:
questions.explanation: The standard $n$-sphere is the set of unit vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$, an $n$-dimensional space. It is the boundary of the closed unit $(n+1)$-ball, not the ball itself.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The torus $T^2$ can be described as the quotient of the unit square $[0,1] \times [0,1]$ by:
options: identifying all four edges to a single point.
identifying each pair of opposite edges by a direct (orientation-preserving) gluing.
identifying one pair of opposite edges with a flip and leaving the other pair free.
questions.explanation: Gluing both pairs of opposite edges of the square directly, $(0,y) \sim (1,y)$ and $(x,0) \sim (x,1)$, yields the torus $T^2 \cong S^1 \times S^1$. A single flipped pair would give the Klein bottle or Mobius band.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The Mobius band is obtained from a rectangle by:
options: identifying both pairs of opposite edges directly.
gluing one pair of opposite edges together with a half-twist (a flip).
collapsing one edge to a point.
removing an open disk from its interior.
questions.explanation: The Mobius band glues one pair of opposite edges with a flip, $(0,y) \sim (1, 1-y)$, leaving the other two edges as a single boundary circle. The result is a non-orientable surface with boundary.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The real projective plane $\mathbb{RP}^2$ can be obtained from $S^2$ by:
options: identifying each point $x$ with its antipode $-x$.
gluing two copies along their equators.
collapsing the equator to a point.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{RP}^2 = S^2 / (x \sim -x)$ is the quotient of the sphere by the antipodal map. Equivalently it is the space of lines through the origin in $\mathbb{R}^3$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A space $X$ is connected when:
options: $X$ cannot be written as a union of two disjoint nonempty open sets.
every two points lie in a common compact subset.
questions.explanation: Connectedness means $X$ admits no separation into two disjoint nonempty open sets. Equivalently, the only subsets that are both open and closed are $\varnothing$ and $X$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is compact if and only if (Heine-Borel):
questions.explanation: The Heine-Borel theorem characterizes compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ as exactly the closed and bounded ones. This characterization is special to $\mathbb{R}^n$ and fails in general metric spaces.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: In the quotient topology on $X / {\sim}$ with projection $q : X \to X/{\sim}$, a set $U \subseteq X/{\sim}$ is open exactly when:
options: $U$ contains the image of a basepoint.
questions.explanation: The quotient topology is the finest topology making $q$ continuous: $U$ is open in the quotient precisely when its preimage $q^{-1}(U)$ is open in $X$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Which of the following spaces is homeomorphic to the circle $S^1$?
options: The boundary of a square (a square loop) in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
questions.explanation: The boundary of a square is a single closed loop homeomorphic to $S^1$. The interval is not (removing an interior point disconnects it but never disconnects $S^1$), and the figure-eight has a point whose removal leaves four ends.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The product topology on $X \times Y$ has as a basis the sets of the form:
options: all subsets $A \times B$ with $A \subseteq X$, $B \subseteq Y$.
$U \times V$ with $U$ open in $X$ and $V$ open in $Y$.
questions.explanation: A basis for the product topology is given by products $U \times V$ of an open set in each factor. Open sets are unions of such basic boxes.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A continuous bijection from a compact space onto a Hausdorff space is automatically:
options: an open embedding into a larger space.
questions.explanation: If $X$ is compact and $Y$ is Hausdorff, a continuous bijection $f : X \to Y$ is a closed map (closed subsets of $X$ are compact, their images are compact hence closed in $Y$), so $f^{-1}$ is continuous and $f$ is a homeomorphism.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Which statement about the standard surfaces is TRUE?
options: The Mobius band has empty boundary.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{RP}^2$ is a compact surface without boundary (closed) and is non-orientable. The torus is orientable, the Mobius band has a single boundary circle, and $S^2$ has no boundary.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The continuous image of a connected space is:
questions.explanation: Connectedness is preserved by continuous maps: if $f : X \to Y$ is continuous and $X$ is connected, then $f(X)$ is connected. A separation of $f(X)$ would pull back to a separation of $X$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A Hausdorff ($T_2$) space is one in which:
options: the only open sets are $\varnothing$ and $X$.
every open cover has a finite subcover.
any two distinct points can be separated by disjoint open sets.
questions.explanation:
The Hausdorff condition states that for distinct points $x 
eq y$ there exist disjoint open sets $U 
i x$ and $V 
i y$. Every subset open is the discrete topology, and the finite-subcover property is compactness.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The open interval $(0,1)$ is homeomorphic to:
questions.explanation: The map $x \mapsto \tan(\pi(x - 1/2))$ is a homeomorphism $(0,1) \to \mathbb{R}$. The closed interval is compact while $(0,1)$ is not, and $S^1$ is compact too, so neither is homeomorphic to $(0,1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Identifying the entire boundary circle of a closed disk $D^2$ to a single point produces a space homeomorphic to:
questions.explanation: Collapsing $\partial D^2$ to a point gives $D^2 / \partial D^2 \cong S^2$. More generally $D^n / \partial D^n \cong S^n$, a standard quotient description of the sphere.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT generally a topological property (invariant under homeomorphism)?
questions.explanation: Completeness depends on the metric, not just the topology: $(0,1)$ and $\mathbb{R}$ are homeomorphic, yet $\mathbb{R}$ is complete in its usual metric while $(0,1)$ is not. Connectedness, compactness, and Hausdorffness are genuine topological invariants.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The wedge sum $X \vee Y$ of two pointed spaces is formed by:
options: taking the disjoint union with no identifications.
gluing $X$ and $Y$ together at their basepoints.
questions.explanation: The wedge sum $X \vee Y$ is the quotient of the disjoint union $X \sqcup Y$ in which the two basepoints are identified to one point. For example $S^1 \vee S^1$ is the figure-eight.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Path-connectedness implies connectedness, but the converse:
options: is true only for finite spaces.
fails in general (some connected spaces are not path-connected).
questions.explanation: Every path-connected space is connected, but the converse can fail. The topologist's sine curve is the standard example of a connected space that is not path-connected.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Gluing two disks $D^2$ along their boundary circles by the identity map produces:
questions.explanation: Two disks joined along their common boundary circle form a sphere: think of the northern and southern hemispheres glued along the equator. This realizes $S^2$ as a CW complex with two 2-cells.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: An embedding of $X$ into $Y$ is a continuous injection $f : X \to Y$ such that:
options: $f$ is a homeomorphism onto its image $f(X)$ with the subspace topology.
questions.explanation: An embedding is a map that is a homeomorphism onto its image, where the image carries the subspace topology. A continuous injection alone is not enough; the inverse on the image must also be continuous.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Recall how the standard surfaces are built and which are orientable.
questions.solution: The torus is the quotient of a square with both pairs of opposite edges glued directly; as a product it is $S^1 \times S^1$, and it {{1}} orientable. The real projective plane $\mathbb{RP}^2$ is the sphere $S^2$ with antipodal points identified, and it {{2}} orientable. Counting boundary circles, the number of boundary circles of a Mobius band is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The torus $S^1 \times S^1$ is orientable. The projective plane is non-orientable. The Mobius band has exactly $1$ boundary circle (its single edge is one connected loop).
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Decide compactness for subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ using Heine-Borel, then count.
questions.solution: By Heine-Borel, a subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is compact exactly when it is closed and bounded. The closed interval $[0,1]$ {{1}} compact, while the real line $\mathbb{R}$ is closed but unbounded, so it {{2}} compact. Among the three sets $[0,1]$, $(0,1)$, and $\mathbb{R}$, the number that are compact is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $[0,1]$ is closed and bounded, hence compact. $(0,1)$ is not closed and $\mathbb{R}$ is not bounded, so neither is compact. Exactly $1$ of the three is compact.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Identify the quotient of a disk by collapsing its boundary, in two dimensions.
questions.solution: Collapsing the boundary circle of the closed disk $D^2$ to a single point yields the space {{1}}. In general $D^n / \partial D^n$ is an $n$-sphere, so the dimension of the sphere obtained from $D^2$ this way is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $D^2 / \partial D^2 \cong S^2$, an $n$-sphere with $n = 2$. The general pattern is $D^n / \partial D^n \cong S^n$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Recall the dimensions of the standard spheres as subsets of Euclidean space.
questions.solution: The sphere $S^n$ sits inside $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ as the unit vectors. So the circle $S^1$ lives in $\mathbb{R}^k$ with $k =$ {{1}}, and the ordinary $2$-sphere $S^2$ lives in $\mathbb{R}^m$ with $m =$ {{2}}. The dimension of $S^2$ as a manifold is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $S^n \subset \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$, so $S^1 \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ and $S^2 \subset \mathbb{R}^3$. The $2$-sphere is a $2$-dimensional manifold.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology
